note: Channel is 9 days old (started 2026-06-09). 28 videos total, 20 matured retention, 8 retention-pending (france-senegal, norway-iraq, argentina-algeria, austria-jordan, portugal-dr-congo, england-croatia, ghana-panama, colombia-uzbekistan). 4 new videos matured since last analysis: spain-cabo-verde (84.57% avg_view — exceptional), belgium-egypt (56.81%), uruguay-saudi (57.84% + 11 comments), iran-nz (59.74%). The Cabo Verde video is a critical data point: it proves post_match CAN hold at 84.57% when framed as the giant's failure + underdog story rather than a chronological goal timeline. Subscribe_scene gap narrowing: sweden-tunisia and uruguay-saudi still lack subscribe scenes (combined ~7,600 views). germany-curacao and belgium-egypt had subscribe scenes but got 0 subs — the scene alone isn't enough. New england-croatia (player_story, 5 comments) and portugal-dr-congo (player_story, 3 comments) show contestable questions on narrative formats drive comments.

learnings.finding: Overall subscribe conversion is 0.11% (47 subs from ~41k matured views). Two high-reach videos STILL lack subscribe scenes: sweden-tunisia (6,312 views, 3 subs = 0.048%) and uruguay-saudi (1,275 views, 0 subs). germany-curacao and belgium-egypt HAD subscribe scenes and got 0 subs each — the scene alone isn't enough; the ask is too generic/end-card-only. The sweden-tunisia miss alone likely cost the channel 3-6 subs at baseline conversion.
learnings.proposed_rule_change: EVERY video MUST have has_subscribe_scene=true. But also: move the subscribe ask mid-roll (scene 2-3) with a specific reason ('subscribe to follow [subject]'s World Cup'), not just the end-card. End-card-only doesn't convert even when present.

learnings.finding: post_match format dominates reach: netherlands-japan-comeback 13.3k, sweden-tunisia 6.3k, ivory-coast 2.1k, iran-nz 1.8k, argentina-algeria 1.7k, norway-iraq 1.5k, france-senegal 1k. The median post_match avg_view_pct among 7 matured is 59.69% — near channel median. BUT: the spain-cabo-verde video (84.57% avg_view, N=1) proves post_match CAN hold exceptionally well when framed as the giant's failure + underdog story rather than a chronological goal timeline. The pattern is now less 'post_match bleeds' and more 'standard timeline post_match bleeds; underdog/failure-framed post_match holds.'
learnings.proposed_rule_change: Keep post_match as the reach driver. Frame the match around ONE person's failure/redemption or the underdog's moment, not a chronological timeline. The Cabo Verde template (hook = 'Spain couldn't score', beat 2 = 'their first World Cup') is the model. Always set has_subscribe_scene. Target <=28s.

learnings.finding: 10 of 20 matured videos (50%) have their biggest retention drop at scene 2 — the first body beat after the hook. Down from 63% (10/16), but the videos that ESCAPE scene-2 leakage are consistently the best holders: caboverde (84.57%, leak at scene 5), messi (100.66%, scene 1), germany-curacao (81.06%, scene 1). Videos that leak at scene 2: all below or near channel median (avg ~57-59%). Scene 2 fails when it hands the story to the opponent ('EGYPT STRUCK FIRST', 'SAUDI ARABIA STRUCK FIRST', 'CZECHIA STRUCK FIRST'). It succeeds when it escalates the hook's stakes.
learnings.proposed_rule_change: Scene 2 is the single biggest retention lever. Never open scene 2 with the opponent scoring or with a downbeat stat. Every first body beat must BUILD on the hook, not deflate it. The Cabo Verde proof: scene 2 = 'THEIR FIRST WORLD CUP' (escalates the hook about Spain's failure).

learnings.finding: The best HOLDERS are single-subject rising arcs: messi-last-dance (100.66%, 0.401 end), germany-curacao-news_story (81.06%, 0.264 end), spain-cabo-verde (84.57%, 0.290 end — framed as Cape Verde's moment), balogun-brace (71.41%, 0.221 end). The worst holders are multi-beat match narratives: korea-czechia (48.42%), netherlands-japan-preview (47.95%). The pattern is consistent: pick the surprise hero or the giant's failure, not a timeline.
learnings.proposed_rule_change: Frame match stories as one person's arc or one team's shock moment through the match. Pick the surprise hero (not the obvious star). Never let the first body beat hand the result away.

learnings.finding: post_match format still near-zero comments: 13 of 15 post_match videos have <0.1% comment rate. The ONE outlier remains uruguay-saudi (11 comments, 0.86%) using a contestable group-debate question ('who's escaping Group H?'). england-croatia (5 comments, 0.42%) and portugal-dr-congo (3 comments, 0.19%) are new player_story entries with contestable questions — both promising. The mechanic is provocation, not format.
learnings.proposed_rule_change: Never end a post_match recap with a yes/no or soft question. Use a contestable, specific debate: name the teams, ask for a pick, frame a disagreement. The uruguay-saudi, england-croatia, and portugal-dr-congo videos all prove the mechanic works when the prompt is contestable.

learnings.finding: With 20 matured videos, the pattern holds: best hook survival is on videos with a SPECIFIC, QUANTIFIED deprivation about the SUBJECT: pulisic ('27 games without a goal'), messi ('record sixth World Cup'), balogun ('nobody trusted USA's striker'), spain/cabo-verde ('90 minutes. Zero goals.' — the giant's failure). Worst: generic hooks or hooks opening on the opponent's success ('TROPHY FAVORITES — THEN A SHOCK'). The Cabo Verde video confirms the pattern: specific quantification of Spain's failure ('90 minutes. No goals') + underdog frame holds 84.57%.
learnings.proposed_rule_change: First frame = a specific, quantified, unresolved fact about the SUBJECT (or the giant's quantified failure). Never open on the opponent's goal or achievement. Scene 2 must escalate (not deflate) the hook.

learnings.finding: The gap between best and worst holders is 53 percentage points (Messi 100.66% vs netherlands-japan-preview 47.95%). Within the SAME format (post_match), caboverde holds 84.57% and belgium-egypt holds 56.81%. The driver is execution — specifically whether the hook + scene 2 escalation frames a single narrative (underdog/giant failure) or a chronological timeline.
learnings.proposed_rule_change: Do not trust format to carry retention. Audit every scene 2: does it escalate the hook or hand the story to the opponent? Build a tight rise through the first 3 scenes around ONE person's arc or ONE team's shock moment.

learnings.finding: TWO leak stages confirmed: (1) impression-level swipe-away (estimated ~60-70%, not measurable from Analytics API). (2) AMONG counted views past the start, scene 2 is still the biggest drop (50% of videos), then a gradual body bleed. Retained_to_end ranges 0.080-0.401 (median 0.149). 4 videos now leak at scene 5 (group_table scenes) — this may be viewers interpreting the table as 'video is over' before the end card.
learnings.proposed_rule_change: Fix the body: scene 2 is the single biggest lever — stop deflating the hook there. For group_table scenes, consider whether they should go BEFORE the end card or whether the end card needs a stronger re-engagement hook.

learnings.finding: quiz_top5 remains the worst-reaching format: mean ~381 views vs ~1.3k+ for post_match recaps. Quiz retention is mid-pack (avg 63.57-63.59%). The problem is click appeal, not holding. Quizzes still lead in comment rate (1.72-1.97%) but the group-debate post_match bait and player_story contestable questions prove that's a provocation mechanic, not format-specific.
learnings.proposed_rule_change: Keep quiz as rare seasoning with a contestable hook. Graft the quiz comment-engine mechanic (contestable question) onto narrative formats instead of making quiz videos.

learnings.finding: All new videos are 22-29s rendered duration (none exceed 30s). The post_match format that drives the most reach reliably stays <=30s. Above 35s (the 56s quizzes) the views crater. Evidence now at n=18.

Pattern continues: all 8 new post_match videos name both countries in the title with the scoreline. The reach floor remains the non-country-naming quizzes (355, 408). Still confounded with format but costs nothing and correlates with every high-reach video.
learnings.proposed_rule_change: For any match video, put BOTH nations' names in the youtube_title (front-loaded with the scoreline).

learnings.finding: SUPERSEDED by post-match-comment-desert (the mechanic is provocation, not quiz format). The uruguay-saudi post_match (11 comments), england-croatia player_story (5 comments), and portugal-dr-congo player_story (3 comments) all prove the same contestable-question mechanic works on narrative formats.
SUPERSEDED by retention-is-execution-not-format and single-subject-arc-holds-better. Execution drives retention, not format label.
